title: Who Would Be Emperor If the Roman Empire Still Existed Today?
description:
in History, Politics |   	March 10th, 2026  6 Comments
	
			

	

DurôÙing WimôÙbleôÙdon a few years ago, a thread about King Felipe VI of Spain went vi
body:
in History, Politics |   	March 10th, 2026  6 Comments
	
			

	

DurôÙing WimôÙbleôÙdon a few years ago, a thread about King Felipe VI of Spain went viral. It was postôÙed to the social media platôÙform forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter by Derek Guy, author of the menswear blog Die, WorkôÙwear! ãVery rare to see this levôÙel of taiôÙlorôÙing nowaôÙdays, even on the wealthy,ã he comôÙmentôÙed on a phoôÙto of Felipe in the stands on the tourôÙnaôÙmenôÙtãs last day. Even when not attendôÙing major sportôÙing events, the kingãs colôÙlars always hug his neck, his lapels are always well-proôÙporôÙtioned, the lines of his coat always flow into his trousers, and his four-in-hand always has just the right asymôÙmeôÙtry. For my monôÙey, such self-preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion befits not just a monarch, but indeed an emperôÙor.
It so hapôÙpens that Felipe is one of the most plauôÙsiôÙble canôÙdiôÙdates for that job, at least in the hypoôÙthetôÙiôÙcal sceôÙnario that the Roman Empire nevôÙer declined and fell. Heãs also the only actuôÙal sitôÙting monarch among them, though each of the othôÙers can also make his own credôÙiôÙble claim to the impeôÙrôÙiôÙal throne.
So who would rightôÙfulôÙly rule over a still-extant Roman Empire? UnderôÙstandôÙing that hisôÙtoôÙry buffs enjoy nothôÙing more than a specôÙuôÙlaôÙtive but knowlôÙedge- and judgôÙment-intenôÙsive debate of that kind, UseôÙfulCharts creôÙator Matt BakôÙer (whose online store hapôÙpens to offer a Roman emperôÙors famôÙiôÙly tree poster) once invitôÙed thirôÙteen hisôÙtoôÙry YouTuôÙbers to cast their votes ã and, of course, explain their answers.
In addiôÙtion to Felipe, the rosôÙter of potenôÙtial modôÙern-day Roman emperôÙors includes Dû¥nôÙdar Ali Osman, heir to the Ottoman dynasty, and Andrew Romanov, heir to the RussôÙian throne (a choice for those who accept the oneôÙtime descripôÙtion of Moscow as the ãthird Romeã). Alas, both have died since the makôÙing of this video, but the claimants who could draw their legitôÙiôÙmaôÙcy from the legaôÙcy of the Holy Roman Empire live on: the still relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly young Jean-Christophe Napolûˋon, a descenôÙdant of BonaôÙparteãs brothôÙer, and Karl von HabôÙsôÙburg, the undisôÙputôÙed curôÙrent head of the eponyôÙmous house. In favor of each canôÙdiôÙdate, one can make a variôÙety of arguôÙments politôÙiôÙcal, culôÙturôÙal, and geoôÙgraphôÙiôÙcal. Nor, as some of us would insist, can we reaôÙsonôÙably ignore the sarôÙtoôÙrôÙiôÙal.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Every Roman EmperôÙor: A Video TimeôÙline MovôÙing from AugusôÙtus to the ByzanôÙtine Empireãs Last Ruler, ConôÙstanôÙtine XI
What Did the Roman EmperôÙors Look Like?: See PhoôÙtoôÙreôÙalôÙisôÙtic PorôÙtraits CreôÙatôÙed with Machine LearnôÙing
Ancient Roman Coins Reveal the ExisôÙtence of a ForôÙgotôÙten Roman EmperôÙor
Five HardôÙcore Deaths SufôÙfered By Roman EmperôÙors
All of the Rulers of Europe Over the Past 2,400 Years PreôÙsentôÙed in a TimeôÙlapse Map (400 B.C. to 2017 A.D.)
The HisôÙtoôÙry of Europe from 400 BC to the Present, AniôÙmatôÙed in 12 MinôÙutes
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The Futurist Cookbook (1930) Tried to Turn Italian Cuisine into Modern Art
description: With the savôÙage cuts in arts fundôÙing, perôÙhaps weãll return to a sysôÙtem of noblesse oblige familôÙiar to stuôÙdents of The GildôÙed Age, when art
body:
With the savôÙage cuts in arts fundôÙing, perôÙhaps weãll return to a sysôÙtem of noblesse oblige familôÙiar to stuôÙdents of The GildôÙed Age, when artists needôÙed indeôÙpenôÙdent wealth or patronôÙage, and wealthy indusôÙtriôÙalôÙists often decidôÙed what was art, and what wasnãt.ô Unlike fine art, howôÙevôÙer, haute cuiôÙsine has always relied on the patronôÙage of wealthy donorsãor dinôÙers. It can be marôÙketôÙed in preôÙmade pieces, sold in cookôÙbooks, and made to look easy on TV, but for reaôÙsons both culôÙturôÙal and pracôÙtiôÙcal, givôÙen the nature of food, anô exquisôÙiteôÙly-preôÙpared dish can only be made accesôÙsiôÙble to a select few.
Still, we would be misôÙtakôÙen, sugôÙgestôÙed FuturôÙist poet and theôÙoôÙrist F.T. MarinetôÙtiô (1876ã1944), should weô neglect to see cookôÙing as an art form akin to all the othôÙers in its moral and intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙalô influôÙence on us. While hardôÙly the first or the last artist to pubôÙlish a cookôÙbook, Marinettiãs FuturôÙist CookôÙbook seems at first glance deadôÙly, even aggresôÙsiveôÙly, seriôÙous, lackôÙing the whimôÙsy, impracôÙtiôÙcal weirdôÙness, and surôÙreôÙalôÙist art of SalôÙvador Daliãs Les DinôÙers de Gala, for examôÙple,ô or the eclecôÙtic wistôÙfulôÙness of the MoMAãs Artistãs CookôÙbook.
Just as he had sought with his earôÙliôÙer FuturôÙist ManôÙiôÙfestoô to revôÙoôÙluôÙtionôÙize art, MarinetôÙti intendôÙed his cookôÙbook to foment a ãrevôÙoôÙluôÙtion of cuiôÙsine,ã as Alex RevôÙelôÙli SoriôÙni and SusanôÙna CutiôÙni point out. You might even call it an act of war when it came to cerôÙtain staôÙples of ItalôÙian eatôÙing, like pasôÙta, which he thought responôÙsiôÙble for ãslugôÙgishôÙness, pesôÙsimism, nosôÙtalôÙgic inacôÙtivôÙiôÙty, and neuôÙtralôÙismã (anticôÙiôÙpatôÙing scadsô of low and no-carb diets to come).
BelievôÙing that peoôÙple ãthink, dream and act accordôÙing to what they eat and drink,ã MarinetôÙti forôÙmuôÙlatôÙed strict rules not only for the prepaôÙraôÙtion of food, but also the servôÙing and eatôÙing of it, going so far as to call for abolôÙishôÙing the knife and fork. A short excerpt from his introôÙducôÙtion shows himô applyôÙing to food the techôÙno-romanôÙtiôÙcism of his FuturôÙist theoryãan ethos takôÙen up by BenôÙiôÙto MusôÙsoliôÙni, whom MarinetôÙti supôÙportôÙed:
The FuturôÙist culiôÙnary revôÙoôÙluôÙtion ãÎ has the lofty, noble and uniôÙverôÙsalôÙly expeôÙdiôÙent aim of changôÙing radôÙiôÙcalôÙly the eatôÙing habits of our race, strengthôÙenôÙing it, dynamizôÙing it and spirôÙiôÙtuôÙalôÙizôÙing it with brand-new food comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtions in which experôÙiôÙment, intelôÙliôÙgence and imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtion will ecoôÙnomôÙiôÙcalôÙly take the place of quanôÙtiôÙty, banalôÙiôÙty, repôÙeôÙtiôÙtion and expense.
In hindôÙsight, the fasôÙcist overôÙtones in Marinettiãs lanôÙguage seem glarôÙing. In 1932, when theô FuturôÙist CookôÙbook was pubôÙlished, his FuturôÙism seemed like a much-needôÙed ãjolt to all the pracôÙtiôÙcal and intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal activôÙiôÙties,ã note SoriôÙni and CutiôÙni. ô ãThe subôÙject [of cookôÙing] needôÙed a good shake to reawakôÙen its spirôÙit.ã And thatãs just what it got. The FuturôÙist CookôÙbook actôÙedô as ãa preôÙview of ItalôÙian-style NouôÙvelle CuiôÙsine,ã with such innoôÙvaôÙtions as ãaddiôÙtives and preserôÙvôÙaôÙtives added to food, or using techôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal tools in the kitchen to mince, pulôÙverôÙize, and emulôÙsiôÙfy.ã
Yet, for all the high seriôÙousôÙness with which MarinetôÙtiô seems to treat his subôÙject, ãwhat the media missedã at the time, writes Maria PopoôÙva, ãwas that the cookôÙbook was arguably the greatôÙest artisôÙtic prank of the twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry.ãô In an introôÙducôÙtion to the 1989 ediôÙtion, British jourôÙnalôÙist and hisôÙtoôÙriôÙanô LesôÙley ChamôÙberôÙlain called theô FuturôÙist CookôÙbook ãa seriôÙous joke, revôÙoôÙluôÙtionôÙary in the first instance because it overôÙturned with ribôÙald laughôÙter everyôÙthing ãfoodã and ãcookôÙbooksã held sacred.ã MarinetôÙti first swept away traôÙdiôÙtion in favor of creôÙative dinôÙing events the FuturôÙists called ãaerôÙobanôÙquets,ã such as one in Bologna in 1931 with a table shaped like an airôÙplane and dishôÙes called ãspicy airôÙportã (OliviôÙer salôÙad) and ãrisôÙing thunôÙderã (orange risotôÙto). LamôÙbrôÙusôÙco wine was served in gas cans.
Itãs perôÙforôÙmance art worôÙthy of DalôÙiãs bizarre cosôÙtumed dinôÙner parôÙties, but fueled by a genôÙuine desire to revôÙoôÙluôÙtionôÙize food, if not the actuôÙal eatôÙing of it, by ãbringôÙing togethôÙer eleôÙments sepôÙaôÙratôÙed by biasôÙes that have no true founôÙdaôÙtion.ã So remarked French chef Jules MainôÙcave, a 1914 conôÙvert to FuturôÙism and inspiôÙraôÙtion for what MarinetôÙti calls ãflexôÙiôÙble flaôÙvorôÙful comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtions.ã See sevôÙerôÙal such recipes excerptôÙed from theô FuturôÙist CookôÙbook at Brain PickôÙings, read the full book in ItalôÙian here, and, just below, see Marinettiãs rules for the perôÙfect meal, first pubôÙlished in 1930 as the ãManôÙiôÙfesto of FuturôÙist CuiôÙsine.ã
FuturôÙist cuiôÙsine and rules for the perôÙfect lunch
 1. An origôÙiôÙnal harôÙmoôÙny of the table (crysôÙtal ware, crockôÙery and glassôÙware, decôÙoôÙraôÙtion) with the flaôÙvors and colôÙors of the dishôÙes.
2. Utter origôÙiôÙnalôÙiôÙty in the dishôÙes.
3. The invenôÙtion of flexôÙiôÙble flaôÙvorôÙful comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtions (ediôÙble plasôÙtic comôÙplex), whose origôÙiôÙnal harôÙmoôÙny of form and colôÙor feeds the eyes and awakôÙens the imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtion before temptôÙing the lips.
4. The aboôÙliôÙtion of knife and fork in favor of flexôÙiôÙble comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtions that can delivôÙer prelabiôÙal tacôÙtile enjoyôÙment.
5. The use of the art of perôÙfumery to enhance taste. Each dish must be preôÙcedôÙed by a perôÙfume that will be removed from the table using fans.
6. A limôÙitôÙed use of music in the interôÙvals between one dish and the next, so as not to disôÙtract the senôÙsiôÙtivôÙiôÙty of the tongue and the palate and serves to elimôÙiôÙnate the flaôÙvor enjoyed, restorôÙing a clean slate for tastôÙing.
7. AboôÙliôÙtion of oraôÙtoôÙry and polôÙiôÙtics at the table.
8. MeaôÙsured use of poetôÙry and music as unexôÙpectôÙed ingreôÙdiôÙents to awakôÙen the flaôÙvors of a givôÙen dish with their senôÙsuôÙal intenôÙsiôÙty.
9. Rapid preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion between one dish and the next, before the nosôÙtrils and the eyes of the dinôÙner guests, of the few dishôÙes that they will eat, and othôÙers that they will not, to facilôÙiôÙtate curiosôÙiôÙty, surôÙprise, and imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtion.
10. The creôÙation of simulôÙtaôÙneôÙous and changôÙing morsels that conôÙtain ten, twenôÙty flaôÙvors to be tastôÙed in a few moments. These morsels will also serve the anaôÙlog funcôÙtion [ãÎ] of sumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing an entire area of life, the course of a love affair, or an entire voyôÙage to the Far East.
11. A supôÙply of sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic tools in the kitchen: ozone machines that will impart the scent of ozone to liqôÙuids and dishôÙes; lamps to emit ultraôÙviôÙoôÙlet rays; elecôÙtrolyzôÙers to decomôÙpose extractôÙed juices etc. in order to use a known prodôÙuct to achieve a new prodôÙuct with new propôÙerôÙties; colôÙloidal mills that can be used to pulôÙverôÙize flours, dried fruit and nuts, spices, etc.; disôÙtillôÙing devices using ordiôÙnary presôÙsure or a vacôÙuôÙum, cenôÙtrifuge autoôÙclaves, dialôÙyôÙsis machines. 
The use of this equipôÙment must be sciôÙenôÙtifôÙic, avoidôÙing the error of allowôÙing dishôÙes to cook in steam presôÙsure cookôÙers, which leads to the destrucôÙtion of active subôÙstances (vitôÙaôÙmins, etc.) due to the high temôÙperôÙaôÙtures. ChemôÙiôÙcal indiôÙcaôÙtors will check if the sauce is acidic or basic and will serve to corôÙrect any errors that may occur: lack of salt, too much vineôÙgar, too much pepôÙper, too sweet.ã
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent
When ItalôÙian FuturôÙists Declared War on PasôÙta (1930)
SalôÙvador DalûÙãs 1973 CookôÙbook Gets ReisôÙsued: SurôÙreôÙalôÙist Art Meets Haute CuiôÙsine
MoMAãs Artistsã CookôÙbook (1978) Reveals the Meals of SalôÙvador DalûÙ, Willem de KoonôÙing, Andy Warhol, Louise BourôÙgeois & More
The Artistsã and WritôÙersã CookôÙbook ColôÙlects Recipes From T.C. Boyle, MariôÙna Abramoviá, Neil Gaiman, Joyce CarôÙol Oates & More
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: Inside the Automats Where Coin-Operated Machines Created a Modern, Democratic Dining Experience
description: ãGood evening,ã said Alfred HitchôÙcock to the teleôÙviôÙsion viewôÙers of AmerôÙiôÙca on March 25, 1959. ãTonight Iãm dinôÙing at my favorite club.
body:
ãGood evening,ã said Alfred HitchôÙcock to the teleôÙviôÙsion viewôÙers of AmerôÙiôÙca on March 25, 1959. ãTonight Iãm dinôÙing at my favorite club. There are many advanôÙtages here. As you can see, inforôÙmalôÙiôÙty is the rule. There is also the stimôÙuôÙlaôÙtion of intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal comôÙpanôÙionôÙship withôÙout the deafôÙenôÙing quiôÙet that perôÙvades most clubs. Best of all, I like its priôÙvaôÙcy: only four perôÙsons are allowed at a table, and, of course, no one pays any attenôÙtion to you.ã This was an examôÙple of the deadôÙpan irony with which the filmôÙmakôÙer introôÙduced each broadôÙcast of Alfred HitchôÙcock Presents, for the ãclubã of which he spoke was clearôÙly an automat. Today, many readôÙers under about 50 will nevôÙer have heard the word, butô at the time,ô it referred to a seemôÙingôÙly perôÙmaôÙnent instiôÙtuôÙtion in AmerôÙiôÙcan life.


	

Or rather, an instiôÙtuôÙtion of urban AmerôÙiôÙcan life, and above all in two cities, PhiladelôÙphia and New York. There, no one could think of automats withôÙout thinkôÙing of Horn & Hardart, in its heyôÙday the largest restauôÙrant chain in the world. The conôÙcept, which co-founder Joseph Horn importôÙed over from Berlin in the earôÙly nineôÙteen-tens, was of a restauôÙrant with no waitôÙers: rather, you could choose your dish û  la carte from a wall of coin-operôÙatôÙed comôÙpartôÙments, payôÙing the nickôÙel or two that would allow you to take the food inside.
SalôÙisôÙbury steak,ô creamed spinach, baked beans, a ham-and-cheese sandôÙwich, macôÙaôÙroni and cheese, chocoôÙlate pudôÙding, strawôÙberôÙry rhubarb pie: whatôÙevôÙer it was, the behind-the-scenes staff would replace it just as soon as you put the last one on your tray.


	

Smack of moderôÙniôÙty though it once did (and in a way, still does), the term automat is someôÙwhat misôÙleadôÙing. We might describe the expeôÙriôÙence of visôÙitôÙing one as dinôÙing inside a giant vendôÙing machine, but the actuôÙal runôÙning of the operôÙaôÙtion was quite labor-intenôÙsive. Most of the work was perôÙformed out of the cusôÙtomerãs sight, as far away as in the large cenôÙtral comôÙmisôÙsaries that preôÙpared many of the dishôÙes to be transôÙportôÙed daiôÙly to Horn & Hardartãs 88 locaôÙtions. This sheer scale of operôÙaôÙtion allowed the chain to offer some of the cheapôÙest meals comôÙmerôÙcialôÙly availôÙable, with the result that its automats boomed even ã indeed, espeôÙcialôÙly ã durôÙing the Great DepresôÙsion. Their ecoôÙnomôÙic barôÙriôÙer was low, and of sex and race, nonexôÙisôÙtent; those who rememôÙber them describe them becomôÙing some of the most demoôÙcôÙraôÙtôÙic instiôÙtuôÙtions in postôÙwar AmerôÙiôÙca.


	

You can hear such memôÙoôÙries recalled inô the recent docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaryô The Automatô by figôÙures like Ruth BadôÙer GinsôÙburg, ColôÙin PowôÙell, and Mel Brooks, who rhapôÙsodizes about Horn & Hardartãs cofôÙfee,ô disôÙpensed for just a nickôÙel from elabôÙoôÙrate dolôÙphin-headôÙed spigôÙots. That degree of detail was stanôÙdard in the inteôÙriôÙors, whose marôÙble, chrome, and glass look palaôÙtial by the stanôÙdards of the fast-food joints that ultiôÙmateôÙly replaced the automat. That gloôÙry was one casuôÙalôÙty of postôÙwar subôÙurôÙbanôÙizaôÙtion and holôÙlowôÙing-out of cenôÙtral cities that resultôÙed. What with the AmerôÙiôÙcan urban renaisôÙsance of the past few decades, attempts have been made to revive the automat conôÙcept, but perôÙhaps, as Brooks puts it, ãthe logisôÙtics and the ecoôÙnomôÙics of today wonãt allow anyôÙthing that simôÙple, naû₤ve, and eloôÙquent and beauôÙtiôÙful to flourôÙish again.ã OrderôÙing a meal brought straight to your door may be more conôÙveôÙnient, but even delivôÙery-app addicts have to admit that it will nevôÙer have the same romance.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Slot Machine Age: A 1964 British NewsôÙreel Angsts Over Whether AutoôÙmatôÙed Machines Will DisôÙplace PeoôÙple
How Edward Hopperãs PaintôÙings Inspired the Creepy SusôÙpense of Alfred Hitchcockãs Rear WinôÙdow
Watch the ãBibôÙlio-Matã Book-VendôÙing Machine DisôÙpense LitôÙerôÙary Delight
Behold the Art-o-Mat: VinôÙtage CigôÙaôÙrette VendôÙing Machines Get RepurôÙposed & DisôÙpense Works of Art
How Fast Food Began: The HisôÙtoôÙry of This ThorôÙoughôÙly AmerôÙiôÙcan (and Now GlobôÙal) Form of DinôÙing
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: AI Figures Out the Rules of a Mysterious 2,000-Year-Old Board Game from Ancient Rome
description:
in Games, History |   	March 9th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by WalôÙter Crist
As far as enthuôÙsiôÙasm for board games goes, no conôÙtiôÙnent has
body:
in Games, History |   	March 9th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Image by WalôÙter Crist
As far as enthuôÙsiôÙasm for board games goes, no conôÙtiôÙnent has yet outôÙdone Europe. Its advanôÙtage could lie in the highôÙly develôÙoped culôÙture of low-cost leisure eviôÙdent in quite a few of its sociôÙeties; it could also owe to the fact that board games seem to have been played there conôÙtinôÙuôÙousôÙly since antiqôÙuiôÙty. Weãve long had eviôÙdence of examôÙples like the ãRoman mill game,ã betôÙter known today as nine menãs morôÙris, which Ovid appears to menôÙtion in his Ars AmaôÙtoôÙriaô of the very earôÙly first cenôÙtuôÙry. Not that modôÙern knowlôÙedge of Roman tableôÙtop gamôÙing is comôÙplete. In one puzôÙzling case, the stone board above was unearthed in a forôÙmer Roman town in the NetherôÙlands, but how a game was played on it remained a mysôÙtery ã until machine learnôÙing came along.
ãTo examôÙine whether the object may have been used as a game board, we perôÙformed use-wear analyôÙsis to idenôÙtiôÙfy eviôÙdence for gameôÙplay and we simôÙuôÙlatôÙed play using artiôÙfiôÙcial intelôÙliôÙgence (AI),ã write the team of researchers who recentôÙly pubôÙlished a paper on the subôÙject inô the jourôÙnalô AntiqôÙuiôÙty. They used a sysôÙtem called Ludii, engiôÙneered to anaôÙlyze board-game rules.
ãThis softôÙware allows for AI-driôÙven playôÙout simôÙuôÙlaôÙtion, where two AI agents play a game against one anothôÙer, which can genôÙerôÙate quanôÙtiôÙtaôÙtive data on gameôÙplay. In this instance, we explored whether the rules of a game would proôÙduce the wear patôÙtern seen on the stone.ã The idea, in othôÙer words, was to let the comôÙputôÙer play against itself using difôÙferôÙent rules until it came upon a game that would conôÙtinôÙue to abrade away the surôÙface of the board in the same fashôÙion as it already was.
This process narôÙrowed it down to games ãin which the goal is to block the oppoôÙnent from movôÙing, and those in which the goal is to place three pieces in a row.ã These have a fairôÙly long docôÙuôÙmentôÙed hisôÙtoôÙry, from ScanôÙdiôÙnaviôÙaãs hareôÙtavl, to Italyãs gioôÙco dellãorsoô to Spainãs liebre perseguiôÙda, to Greeceãs kinûˋôÙgi tou lagoû¤. You can downôÙload what the research sugôÙgests is the most plauôÙsiôÙble rule set for this parôÙticôÙuôÙlar Roman board game here, board design includôÙed. One playôÙer takes the side of the ãhunter,ã with four pieces, and the othôÙer the side of the ãprey,ã with two. The forôÙmer tries to trap the latôÙterãs pieces, movôÙing only along the boardôÙãs lines; in the next round, the roles reverse. The hunter who does the job in the fewest moves wins. Why not invite friends over to spend an evening playôÙing like a Roman? For a thorôÙoughôÙly ancient good time, first reconôÙstruct as best you can the ambiôÙence of the therôÙmopoliôÙumô at home.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Watch a Playthrough of the OldôÙest Board Game in the World, the SumerôÙian RoyôÙal Game of Ur, CirôÙca 2500 BC
Behold the First AmerôÙiôÙcan Board Game, TravôÙellersã Tour Through the UnitôÙed States (1822)
MonopôÙoly: How the OrigôÙiôÙnal Game Was Made to ConôÙdemn MonopôÙoôÙlies & the AbusôÙes of CapôÙiôÙtalôÙism
Kurt Vonnegutãs Lost Board Game Is FinalôÙly for Sale
The FiendishôÙly ComôÙpliôÙcatôÙed Board Game That Takes 1,500 Hours to Play: DisôÙcovôÙer The CamôÙpaign for North Africa
The Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas Board Game, Inspired by Hunter S. Thompsonãs RolôÙlickôÙing NovôÙel
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Lost for More Than a Century, the First 'Sci-Fi' Film Ever Made Resurfaces
description: Around 1897, the French director Georges Mûˋliû´s made a silent short film that, until last month, hadnãt been publicly viewable for more than a cen
body:
Around 1897, the French director Georges Mûˋliû´s made a silent short film that, until last month, hadnãt been publicly viewable for more than a century. ãGugusse et lãAutomate,ã or ãGugusse and the Automaton,ã is a 45-second slapstick piece featuring a magician and a Pierrot-styled robot as they duke it out. 
Mûˋliû´s is best known for ãA Trip to the Moon,ã a short film from 1902 that famously features astromoners landing their capsule into the eye of the moon. The directorãs work is widely regarded as some of the first within fantasy and science fiction, with ãGugusse et lãAutomateã being a long-lost addition to his canon.



This film resurfaced recently when Bill McFarland drove from his Grand Rapids, Michigan-home to the Libraryãs National Audio-Visual Conservation Center in Culpeper, Virginia, with a cache of reels that once belonged to his great-grandfather, William Delisle Frisbee. Passed down through the family, this collection was part of Frisbeeãs traveling showbusiness, in which he packed up his horse and buggy in western Pennsylvania and traveled to nearby towns to screen these early ãmoving picturesã accompanied by music from a phonograph.
According to the library, McFarlandãs copy of ãGugusse et lãAutomateã is ãa duplicate at least three times removed from the original. Library technicians spent more than a week scanning and stabilizing it onto a digital format, so that it can now be seen by anyone onlineãin 4K, no less.ã
The collection also contained Mûˋliû´sã ãThe Fat and Lean Wrestling Matchã and parts of Thomas Edisonãs ãThe Burning Stable.ã See more of conservatorsã unraveling process on Instagram. (via Kottke)

schema.headline: Lost for More Than a Century, the First 'Sci-Fi' Film Ever Made Resurfaces

title: An Animated Look at Noguchi's Experimental Playgrounds That Were Never Built
description: ãI think of playgrounds as a primer of shapes and functions; simple, mysterious and evocative; thus educational,ã Isamu Noguchi said in a pamphlet
body:
ãI think of playgrounds as a primer of shapes and functions; simple, mysterious and evocative; thus educational,ã Isamu Noguchi said in a pamphlet about his Playscapes. Perhaps best known for his stone sculptures and Akari lamps, the Japanese artist and designer always had an eye on the spaces that define childhood, particularly public playgrounds and their influence on the young mind.
In 1933, Noguchi proposed redeveloping an entire New York City block into ãPlay Mountain,ã an enormous topographical project that would be unstructured and open-ended. Rather than have swings and swift metal slides, for example, Noguchi wanted earthen steps, a bandshell, and a large hill for sledding and gathering. The idea was that it could be just as fun in the winter as in the summer and stimulate kidsã imaginations more than the prescriptive equipment typical in urban parks. Then-Parks Commissioner Robert Moses rejected the plan, though, and despite efforts to have the project and others of Noguchiãs designs built in New York, none were ever realized in the city.



A series of short animations recreates this lesser-known history. Using hand-painted celluloid under a Rostrum camera, Eastend Western imagines what these never-built playgrounds would have looked likeãand how children may have interacted with the unconventional structures. There are concrete mounds with cavernous openings, labyrinthine sand gardens, and asymmetrical equipment that could teach users that ãthe rate of swing is determined by the length of the pendulum,ã the film says.
The animations were produced in conjunction with the exhibition Noguchiãs New York, which is on view through September 13 at The Noguchi Museum. Thereãs also a new monograph that dives into the artistãs playgrounds and is a companion to a major retrospective at the High Museum of Art, available for pre-order on Bookshop. Find the full film series on YouTube.

schema.headline: An Animated Look at Noguchi's Experimental Playgrounds That Were Never Built

title: Mirrors, Iron, and Stone Conjure Ancestral Healing in Olayami Dabls' Detroit Museum
description: Olayami Dabls is careful to call attention to the distinction between material culture and fine art. After working as an artist and curator for th
body:
Olayami Dabls is careful to call attention to the distinction between material culture and fine art. After working as an artist and curator for the Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History in the 1970s, Dabls shifted directions and founded the MBAD African Bead Museum in 1994 to reintroduce African culture and healing into the Detroit community.
The artist had noticed that much of the African American history museumãs collections were regarded with fear and misunderstanding, particularly as they were viewed through a colonial, European lens. With MBAD, Dabls decided to honor ancestral creation and directed his energy to fulfilling ãa need in our community to offer a true experience, free of European constructs and manipulation that furthers cultural differences.ã He focused not on entertainment or profit but rather on the healing power of creation.

What emerged was a massive outdoor project spanning two blocks at the corner of Grand River and West Grand Boulevard. Comprised of dozens of assemblages and installations, MBAD focuses on four materials common throughout formerly industrial Detroit: iron, stone, wood, and mirrored glass. Each had a spiritual power, too. Iron and stone for their connection to our bodies and the Earth, and mirrors for their reflective properties. Wood, too, embodies the strength and grounding qualities of trees.
Perhaps the most recognizable of these works is the ãNkisi House,ã from the Iron Teaching Rocks How to Rust series. Nkisi are objects inhabited by spirits, a concept that originated in the Congo Basin and was brought to the Americas with the transatlantic slave trade. Covered in mirrored shards, painted panels, and other found objects, ãNkisi Houseã is both an alluring structure and a divine invitation.
Dabls considers public art to be one of the underutilized modes of healing in Western cultures, particularly as people gravitate toward treating symptoms rather than rooting out their causes. He believes projects like MBAD can ãtrigger things in people through sight and sound, such as forgotten memories and deep realizations,ã connecting visitors to one another and those who came before. He says:

Our African ancestors used material culture to convey information about themselves, their identity, and to send messages for healing and protection. Material culture was used to share stories from the past to the present. It was used to heal, the same way we go to a doctor or psychiatrist today. Cultural materials like masks, textiles, and carvings communicate a very specific message to educate people, relieve stress, and act as medicine.

Unlike most museums, which separate curation from the gift shop, Dabls considers the bead store and exhibitions as one, offering visitors the opportunity to create within the space and take a piece with them.

Next month at the Museum of Contemporary Art Detroit, Detroit Cosmologies presents 45 years of the artistãs practice, from his site-specific installations to found object assemblages. The retrospective attempts to reframe ãhow museums can hold history, ritual, and community,ã while highlighting how MBAD remains an active, participatory site of cultural creation and memory.
MBAD is also one of several artist environments that began as a grassroots effort in Detroit and is now gaining institutional recognition. Tyree Guytonãs magical Heidelberg Project has seen similar accolades in recent years.
Detroit Cosmologies runs from April 25 to July 12. Find more from MBAD and Dabls on their website.





Olayami Dabls at MBAD African Bead Museum

schema.headline: Mirrors, Iron, and Stone Conjure Ancestral Healing in Olayami Dabls' Detroit Museum
